section.title: A Weaned Soul at Rest
section.sectionRationale: A Song of Ascents of David, twelfth in the pilgrim collection Psalms 120 to 134, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. Among the shortest psalms, it distills a lifetime of hard-won humility into three verses, moving from personal contentment to a call for the whole nation to hope in the LORD.
section.thesis: A humble heart that refuses proud ambition finds a stilled and quieted soul, like a weaned child content in its mother's arms, and calls all God's people to the same settled hope in the LORD, a childlike trust perfectly embodied in Christ's own humility.
section.frameNote: Two movements: a humble, quieted heart before the LORD, verses 1 to 2, and the call for Israel to hope in the LORD forever, verse 3.

verses.text: My heart is not proud, O LORD, my eyes are not haughty. I do not aspire to great things or matters too lofty for me.
Surely I have stilled and quieted my soul; like a {{gamul|weaned child}} with his mother, like a weaned child is my soul within me.

units.label: Put your hope in the LORD

verses.text: O Israel, put your hope in the LORD, both now and forevermore.

gamul.gloss: Weaned; a young child who has moved past need for the breast yet still rests securely against the mother, a picture of settled, dependent trust rather than restless craving, verse 2.

basic.context: Attributed to David, this brief Song of Ascents distills a hard-won lesson in humility, refusing proud ambition and instead finding a stilled, quieted soul like a weaned child resting in its mother's arms, before calling all Israel to the same settled hope in the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes David's own tested humility before the LORD, and it prescribes as an abiding pattern refusing proud ambition, cultivating a quieted soul content with God's provision, and placing hope in the LORD both now and forever.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm's central image is gamul, a weaned child, no longer restless for the breast yet still resting securely against the mother, a picture of mature, settled trust rather than either infantile craving or independent self-sufficiency.
basic.typology: The weaned child's settled trust anticipates the childlike faith Jesus commends as the pattern for entering the kingdom, and David's refusal of proud ambition anticipates Christ's own perfect humility, who did not grasp at equality with God but emptied Himself.
basic.christ: Jesus, though truly great, did not consider equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied Himself, taking the form of a servant, Philippians 2:6-8, the perfect fulfillment of this psalm's refusal of proud ambition, and He calls His people to receive the kingdom like children, Mark 10:15.
basic.god: The LORD is the one before whom pride and lofty ambition are laid down, and He is the secure, mothering presence in whom a soul finds rest, not because all its needs are still being met moment by moment, but because it has learned settled contentment in Him.
basic.crossReferences: Philippians 2:6-8 shows Christ's supreme humility, refusing to grasp at what was rightfully His. Mark 10:15 calls disciples to receive the kingdom of God like a little child. 1 Peter 5:6-7 calls believers to humble themselves and cast every anxiety on God, who cares for them.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's picture of a humble heart and a weaned, settled soul is fulfilled in Christ's own perfect humility, and it calls believers, like Him, to lay down proud ambition and rest in childlike, settled hope in the LORD forever.

context.literary: One of the shortest psalms, only three verses, structured as a negative confession of what the singer will not do, verse 1, a positive image of what the singer has become, verse 2, and a communal exhortation, verse 3.
context.historical: Traditionally connected to David, whose life included both towering ambition and moments of chastened humility, this psalm reads like distilled wisdom learned through hardship rather than youthful idealism.
context.canonical: Follows Psalm 130's cry from the depths of guilt with the settled peace of a soul already reconciled to the LORD, and anticipates the New Testament's call to humility and childlike trust as marks of citizens of the kingdom.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model, for God's people, a deliberate refusal of proud ambition and a cultivated, settled contentment before the LORD, and to call the whole nation to the same enduring hope.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own personal, tested humility. It prescribes as an abiding pattern refusing proud ambition, cultivating a quieted soul content in God's presence, and placing lasting hope in the LORD, for every believer and for the whole people of God.

debates.question: Does 'matters too lofty for me' in verse 1 forbid all ambition, or only a specific kind of proud overreaching?

views.label: A general call to modest contentment
views.case: The psalm's overall tone favors a broad disposition of humility, content with one's God-given place rather than restlessly grasping for more.

views.label: A specific refusal of proud, self-exalting overreach
views.case: David himself pursued great responsibilities as king under God's calling, so the verse likely targets prideful ambition that oversteps one's God-given calling, not diligent pursuit of legitimate vocation.

debates.bearing: Either reading calls believers to examine whether their ambitions flow from humble submission to God's calling or from restless, self-exalting pride.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's central picture is gamul, a weaned child, no longer restless for the breast yet still resting securely against its mother, a Hebrew image of mature, settled trust that neither craves anxiously nor stands independently apart from God.

typology.type: The weaned child settled and quieted, verse 2
typology.fulfillment: The childlike faith Jesus commends as the pattern for entering the kingdom of God, Mark 10:15.

typology.type: The refusal of proud, lofty ambition, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who did not consider equality with God a thing to be grasped but emptied Himself, Philippians 2:6-8.

study.christ: Jesus, though truly equal with God, did not consider that equality something to be grasped, but emptied Himself, taking the form of a servant and humbling Himself even to death on a cross, Philippians 2:6-8, the supreme fulfillment of this psalm's refusal of proud, lofty ambition. He calls His disciples to receive the kingdom of God like a little child, Mark 10:15, echoing the settled, dependent trust this psalm pictures in the weaned child resting against its mother.
study.god: The LORD is the one before whom pride and lofty ambition are laid down, and He is the secure, mothering presence in whom a soul finds rest, not because every immediate craving is satisfied, but because it has learned settled contentment in His care.

crossReferences.note: Christ's supreme humility, refusing to grasp at equality with God, the fulfillment of this psalm's refusal of proud ambition.
Jesus calls disciples to receive the kingdom of God like a little child, echoing this psalm's picture of settled, childlike trust.
Calls believers to humble themselves and cast every anxiety on God, who cares for them, the practical outworking of this psalm's stilled soul.

study.oneStory: This psalm's picture of a humble heart and a weaned, settled soul is fulfilled in Christ's own perfect humility, and it calls believers, like Him, to lay down proud ambition and rest in childlike, enduring hope in the LORD, both now and forevermore.

sermon.title: That's Life!
sermon.why: Begg treats Psalms 129 to 131 together, showing how this psalm's picture of a humble, weaned soul completes the sequence's movement from affliction, through forgiveness, to settled contentment in the LORD.

quiz.q: What does David deny about his own heart and eyes in verse 1?

options.text: That his heart is proud or his eyes haughty.
options.note: This is the exact denial that opens the psalm's confession of humility.

options.text: That he has any love for the LORD.
options.note: The psalm is a confession of humble devotion to the LORD, not a denial of love for Him.

options.text: That he has ever sinned in any way.
options.note: The psalm addresses pride and ambition specifically, not a blanket claim of sinlessness.

options.text: That he has any physical eyesight at all.
options.note: Haughty eyes is a figure of speech for arrogance, not a statement about literal eyesight.

quiz.q: What does David say he does not aspire to, according to verse 1?

options.text: Great things or matters too lofty for him.
options.note: This is the exact phrase describing the ambition David deliberately refuses.

options.text: Any form of daily work or responsibility.
options.note: The psalm addresses proud overreaching, not ordinary work or responsibility.

options.text: A relationship with the LORD.
options.note: The psalm is entirely about David's relationship with the LORD, not a rejection of it.

options.text: Basic provisions like food and shelter.
options.note: The 'lofty matters' in view are things beyond one's place or calling, not basic needs.

quiz.q: What image does verse 2 use to describe David's stilled soul?

options.text: A weaned child resting with its mother.
options.note: This is the exact image the psalm uses for a settled, quieted soul.

options.text: A soldier resting after victorious battle.
options.note: The image is domestic and intimate, a weaned child with its mother, not a soldier's rest.

options.text: A ship anchored safely in a storm.
options.note: The specific image given is a weaned child, not a ship or anchor.

options.text: An eagle soaring high above the clouds.
options.note: The image is one of quiet closeness and rest, not soaring flight.

quiz.q: What does verse 3 call all Israel to do?

options.text: Put their hope in the LORD, both now and forevermore.
options.note: This is the psalm's exact closing exhortation to the whole nation.

options.text: Abandon hope during difficult seasons.
options.note: The verse calls for enduring hope, the opposite of abandoning it.

options.text: Place their hope only in future generations.
options.note: The hope commanded is in the LORD Himself, not merely in future descendants.

options.text: Hope in the LORD only during times of prosperity.
options.note: The phrase 'now and forevermore' calls for hope in every season, not only prosperous ones.

quiz.q: How does Christ fulfill this psalm's refusal of proud, lofty ambition?

options.text: Though equal with God, He did not grasp at that equality but emptied Himself in humility.
options.note: Philippians 2:6-8 shows Christ's supreme humility as the fulfillment of this psalm's refusal of pride.

options.text: He demanded recognition of His full glory from the very beginning of His ministry.
options.note: Philippians 2 describes Christ's self-emptying humility, the opposite of demanding recognition.

options.text: He rejected all forms of authority and rule.
options.note: Christ humbled Himself yet was also exalted and given all authority, not a rejection of rule as such.

options.text: He taught that ambition of any kind is always sinful.
options.note: The concern is proud, self-exalting ambition, not diligent service exercised in humility.

questions: David refuses to aspire to 'great things too lofty' for him. Where might restless ambition be robbing you of contentment in the place God has actually called you?
The image of a weaned child describes a soul no longer frantic but settled and at rest. Would you describe your own soul right now as restless or as quieted before God?
This settled trust was hard won, likely through David's own experiences of hardship and discipline. What season of difficulty has taught you deeper contentment in God rather than in circumstances?
The psalm calls all Israel, not just David, to hope in the LORD 'now and forevermore.' Is your hope in God tied mainly to present circumstances, or does it stretch into an enduring, forever hope?
Christ modeled the ultimate humility, refusing to grasp at what was rightfully His. Where do you need to follow His example and lay down pride or entitlement this week?

translationNotes.note: Haughty eyes is a common Hebrew idiom for arrogance, since a proud posture is often reflected first in the eyes and bearing, Proverbs 6:17.
In the ancient Near East, weaning often occurred around age three, making the image one of a child old enough to have moved past desperate need yet still choosing closeness and rest with its mother.
Now and forevermore mirrors the closing phrase of Psalm 125:2, tying this psalm's personal humility to the same enduring, communal hope in the LORD.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 131 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 124-132). Verse count (3) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 131:1-3. No em or en dashes present in the source text. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's sermon 'That's Life!' (covering Psalms 129-131) at truthforlife.org.
